metadata: A highly acclaimed conveyor-belt sushi chain originating from Hokkaido, Japan. Renowned for its ultra-fresh seafood (especially sourced directly from the Nemuro port), it offers premium, thick-cut nigiri at incredibly accessible prices, though its intense popularity means wait times can exceed an hour. 10th floor of the GinzaNovo shopping mall. Floor plan can be found in docs/ginzanovo_floorplan.pdf

place_name: Kura Sushi Global Flagship Store Asakusa
metadata: 4th floor of the Asakusa ROX shopping mall. Massive 272-seat, high-tech, festival-themed conveyor-belt sushi restaurant

place_name: Kura Sushi Global Flagship Store Harajuku
metadata: This Kura conveyor belt flagship restaurant blends traditional Edo-period design with pop-culture aesthetics. It features exclusive Harajuku-only menu items like sushi crepes and a dedicated sweets stall

Connected to Shibuya Station, the Shibuya Tokyu Foodshow is a massive, high-end depachika (basement food hall). Following an extensive revamp, it is the largest food hall in the area. It is split into dedicated sweets, bakery, gourmet deli, and fresh grocery zones, offering beautifully displayed bento boxes, international foods, and local treats.

Premier, historic retail complex in Tokyo, Japan. Blending awe-inspiring 1930s architecture with modern offerings, it features luxury boutiques, an expansive depachika (basement food hall), specialty stores like the Pokémon Center DX, and the world-renowned Takashimaya Watch Maison.

Tokyo’s premier luxury department store, seamlessly blending over 90 years of history with modern upscale shopping. Located in the heart of Ginza, it features extensive floors of designer fashion, cosmetics, and jewelry, alongside a legendary depachika (basement food hall) renowned for its exquisite gourmet offerings.

place_name: SEIJO ISHII Tokyo Dome LaQua Store
metadata: Upscale, gourmet supermarket famous for high-quality imports, artisanal snacks, and premium ready-to-eat meals. It’s a favorite among locals and event-goers for grabbing premium bento boxes, craft beer, and wine before catching a baseball game or concert.

GINZA SIX is Tokyo’s largest luxury commercial complex, spanning 13 stories in the heart of the prestigious Ginza district. Opened in 2017, it houses over 240 high-end international boutiques, an expansive food hall, the traditional Kanze Noh Theater, and a lush, 4,000-square-meter rooftop garden.

Kuromon Market (Kuromon Ichiba) is a historic 600-meter covered market in Osaka. Known as 'Osaka's Kitchen', it features roughly 170 stalls offering fresh seafood, premium Wagyu beef, and local street food. Visitors can enjoy made-to-order sushi, oysters, and seasonal fruits in a vibrant, bustling atmosphere.

Japan’s 'Food Town', where one can encounter all kinds of traditional Japanese foods. A mixture of wholesale and retail shops, along with numerous restaurants, line the streets, and new culinary trends are born here. Main alleys get so packed that it is difficult to see anything but human heads. Many items are heavily inflated due to the IG/TikTok boom, sometimes reaching up to $70 to $120 for wagyu or sea urchin bowls. Know your budget in advance and be mindful of what you are paying

The Ameyoko Center Building basement market in Ueno, Tokyo, is a vibrant, chaotic wet market known for exotic Asian ingredients. It feels more like a traditional street market in Hong Kong or Thailand than a standard Japanese store.

Ameyoko (Ameya-Yokocho) is a vibrant, bustling open-air market in Tokyo, nestled under the Yamanote Line tracks between Ueno and Okachimachi stations. Born from a post-World War II black market, today it features over 400 energetic stalls offering everything from fresh seafood to cosmetics, streetwear, and street food.
Known as one of Osaka's principal tourist and nightlife areas, the area runs along the Dōtonbori canal from Dōtonboribashi Bridge to Nipponbashi Bridge in the Namba district of the city's Chūō ward
Landmark alleyway featuring a selection of food stalls for simple fare & alcoholic drinks

Hitachino Brewing Lab is a premier craft beer destination operated by Ibaraki's historic Kiuchi Brewery, famed globally for Hitachino Nest beers. It functions as both a taproom and an experimental lab, offering hands-on brewing classes, fresh-hop tastings, and unique seasonal brews on tap.

One of Japan’s largest entertainment complexes in the heart of Kabukicho. Inside are game arcades, immersive attractions, an amusement casino, nightclubs, and observation spaces with city views. A highlight is Shinjuku Kabuki Hall, serving regional Japanese dishes with daily live performances in a lively izakaya-style setting
Ginza Six is a luxury shopping complex located in the Ginza area of Tokyo
The Umeda Sky Building is a two-tower mixed-use skyscraper. It houses the Kuchu Teien Observatory connecting the Tower East and Tower West on the 39th, 40th and rooftop floors

A supersized version of Don Quijote, Japan's most famous discount store chain. Known for their chaotic, treasure-hunt atmosphere, this multi-story location spans hundreds of thousands of square feet and stock almost anything imaginable at highly discounted prices
A legendary Japanese department store chain renowned as a one-stop-shop for DIY, crafts, high-quality stationery, unique home goods, and cosmetics
A legendary Japanese department store chain renowned as a one-stop-shop for DIY, crafts, high-quality stationery, unique home goods, and cosmetics
A legendary Japanese department store chain renowned as a one-stop-shop for DIY, crafts, high-quality stationery, unique home goods, and cosmetics
A legendary Japanese department store chain renowned as a one-stop-shop for DIY, crafts, high-quality stationery, unique home goods, and cosmetics

Nintendo Tokyo is the company’s flagship official store in Japan, located on the 6th floor of the Shibuya Parco department store. It is a premier destination for fans, offering exclusive merchandise, interactive gaming stations, and a massive selection of themed goods from iconic franchises like Mario, Zelda, Animal Crossing, and Splatoon.

Nintendo OSAKA is the region's official, direct-operated flagship store located on the 13th floor of the Daimaru Umeda Department Store in Umeda, Osaka. It offers a massive selection of exclusive character merchandise from popular franchises like Super Mario, Animal Crossing, Splatoon, and The Legend of Zelda.
Shibuya's notable 5-building department store for fashion, cosmetics, home goods, restaurants, more.

Historic department store in Tokyo. Rooted in the Ginza shopping district, it is celebrated for its highly curated luxury fashion boutiques, rotating art exhibitions, and an expansive basement food hall that is a prime destination for gourmet Japanese delicacies, wines, and gifts.

Located in the heart of Tokyo's premier shopping district on the famous Ginza 4-Chome intersection Ginza Mitsukoshi - Where to Shop, Access, Hours & Price, Mitsukoshi Ginza is a historic, luxury department store offering everything from high-end designer fashion and expansive cosmetics floors to an incredible basement food hall

Shinjuku Takashimaya is a mammoth 16-story department store in Tokyo. Located inside Takashimaya Times Square and just a 2-minute walk from JR Shinjuku Station, it offers a premium shopping experience featuring international luxury brands, fashion, a massive depachika food hall, and diverse rooftop dining.
Treasure trove of kitchenware, plastic food samples and crockery

Jimbocho Book Town in central Tokyo is the world's largest used-bookstore district. Located in the Kanda area, it features over 100 specialty bookshops, vintage cafes, and curry houses. It is an ideal destination for finding rare art books, antique maps, Ukiyo-e prints, and out-of-print literature.

Highly curated stationery boutique located on the 5th floor of the Keio Department Store. It provides a refined selection of high-quality Japanese and imported writing instruments, leather goods, and premium paper, offering a convenient, premium shopping experience for creative enthusiasts.

Founded in 1904, Ginza Itoya is Tokyo’s legendary, multi-story stationery haven. Easily recognizable by its giant red paperclip landmark, the flagship features over a dozen themed floors dedicated to fine writing instruments, premium Japanese paper, desk tools, and bespoke customization services for stationery and art enthusiasts alike.

Kyukyodo is one of Japan’s oldest and most prestigious houses, famous for its high-quality incense, calligraphy supplies, and traditional paper goods. Founded in 1663 as a pharmacy, the historic brand has roots in Kyoto and served as the official stationer to the Imperial House of Japan.

Japan Traditional Crafts Aoyama Square in Tokyo is a premier curated gallery and shop dedicated to authentic, centuries-old Japanese handicrafts. It is the only facility in the country where visitors can view and purchase over 100 varieties of traditional, locally made items—from fine ceramics and lacquerware to textiles and woodwork—all in one location.

Fushimi Inari Shrine is an ancient, sprawling Shinto complex in southern Kyoto famous for its thousands of vibrant vermilion torii gates. Founded in 711 AD, it is the head of over 30,000 Inari shrines across Japan. It is dedicated to the Shinto god of rice, agriculture, and business prosperity.

Senso-ji is Tokyo’s oldest and most iconic Buddhist temple, dedicated to Kannon, the bodhisattva of mercy. Established in 645 AD, the complex features a massive Thunder Gate, a towering five-story pagoda, and the bustling Nakamise shopping street, drawing millions of visitors annually.

Meiji Shrine (Meiji Jingu) is a tranquil Shinto sanctuary in the heart of Tokyo, dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken. Completed in 1920, the shrine is nestled within a sprawling, man-made forest of 120,000 trees, offering a peaceful escape from the bustling city streets of Shibuya.

The Tokyo Imperial Palace is the primary residence of the Emperor of Japan. Located in the heart of Chiyoda Ward, this expansive, 1.15-square-kilometer oasis is built upon the historical site of the former Edo Castle and is famed for its sprawling lawns, classical moats, and iconic Nijubashi bridges.

Located in Kyoto’s historic Higashiyama Ward, the Gion Shinbashi Bridge (and the neighboring Tatsumi Bridge) gracefully spans the serene, willow-lined Shirakawa Canal. It is the crown jewel of the Shinbashi district, celebrated as one of Japan's most picturesque, preservation-worthy streets.

Perched on the 53rd floor of the Roppongi Hills Mori Tower in Tokyo, the Mori Art Museum is one of the world's most iconic sky-high contemporary art institutions. It showcases cutting-edge visual arts, architecture, and design while offering sweeping, panoramic views of the Tokyo skyline.

Located in the heart of Ueno Park, the Tokyo Metropolitan Art Museum is Japan’s first public art museum, originally opened in 1926. Known for its rotating exhibitions rather than a permanent collection, it regularly hosts major international retrospectives, contemporary Japanese art shows, and large-scale public exhibitions. The current building, designed by modernist architect Kunio Maekawa, blends concrete modernism with the greenery of Ueno Park, giving the museum a calm, understated atmosphere. Beyond the galleries, it functions as a community-focused 'doorway to art,' with cafés, sculpture spaces, and programs that make it approachable even for casual visitors.

Ghibli Park is an immersive theme park in Nagakute, Japan, that brings the magical worlds of Studio Ghibli films to life through meticulously designed architecture and lush, natural landscapes. Rather than featuring traditional rides, it invites visitors to freely wander and explore environments inspired by beloved movies like My Neighbor Totoro, Spirited Away, and Howl's Moving Castle.

The Ghibli Museum in Mitaka, Tokyo, is a whimsical, interactive tribute to the legendary animation studio Ghibli Museum. Conceived by director Hayao Miyazaki, it blends a children's museum, fine arts, and a theater, inviting visitors to step directly into the magical worlds of films like My Neighbor Totoro and Spirited Away.
Museum featuring an outdoor sculpture park & some indoor exhibits, plus a natural hot spring

The Science Museum in Tokyo is a hands-on, family-friendly destination in Kitanomaru Park. It features interactive exhibits exploring mechanics, robotics, and cutting-edge Japanese industrial technology. While primarily aimed at children, adults and science enthusiasts will find practical, engaging displays.

The Osaka Science Museum is a hands-on, family-friendly destination on Nakanoshima Island. Featuring nearly 200 interactive exhibits across four floors, it explores the themes of Space and Energy" and boasts a world-class planetarium with a massive 26.5-meter dome."

place_name: Museum of Emerging Science and Innovation
metadata: The National Museum of Emerging Science and Innovation (commonly known as Miraikan) is a cutting-edge science and technology center located on Tokyo's Odaiba waterfront. Established by Japan’s Science and Technology Agency, it features interactive exhibits ranging from robotics and space exploration to the global environment and the mysteries of the universe.

The Tokyo National Museum is Japan's oldest and largest national museum. Located in Ueno Park, it houses over 110,000 artifacts—including nearly 90 National Treasures—spanning ancient to modern Japanese and Asian art. The sprawling campus features five distinct buildings, rotating exhibits, and tranquil historic tea houses.

Gion and Southern Higashiyama form the beating heart of traditional Kyoto. This contiguous area is renowned for its preserved wooden machiya townhouses, ancient temples, and as the city’s premier geisha district.

The National Art Center, Tokyo (NACT) is a unique, dynamic museum in the Roppongi district. Designed by renowned architect Kisho Kurokawa, it features a striking wave-like glass facade. Instead of a permanent collection, NACT hosts large-scale rotating temporary exhibitions ranging from historic art to contemporary design.

Ueno Park is a sprawling public park in central Tokyo, Japan, established in 1873 on the former grounds of Kan'ei-ji Temple. Welcoming over 10 million visitors annually, it is one of the city's most popular destinations, celebrated as a cultural hub and a premier spot for springtime cherry blossoms.

Nara Park is a vast, 660-hectare public park in central Nara, Japan, established in 1880. Famous for its 1,200+ free-roaming sika deer considered messengers of the gods, it also houses world-class historic temples like Todai-ji, Kasuga Taisha, and Kofuku-ji.

Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden is a sprawling 144-acre urban oasis in Tokyo, blending three distinct landscaping styles: a traditional Japanese garden, a formal French garden, and an English landscape garden. Formerly an imperial estate, it features over 1,500 cherry trees, a massive botanical greenhouse, and peaceful, expansive lawns.

The Arashiyama Bamboo Grove is one of Kyoto's most iconic and ethereal sights. Walking along its winding pathways, you are enveloped by towering stalks of mōsō bamboo that filter the sunlight and create a serene, almost otherworldly sensory experience.

Lake Kawaguchiko is the most accessible and popular of the Fuji Five Lakes, renowned for its iconic, unobstructed views of Mount Fuji. Located in Yamanashi Prefecture, it serves as a premier hot spring resort and an ideal hub for exploring the mountain's surrounding attractions.
Ancient pilgrimage routes through cedar forests connecting shrines and tiny onsen towns
An active volcanic landscape that feels almost Icelandic. Grasslands, steaming vents, massive crater walls

Kitayamazaki Cape, located in Tanohata, Iwate Prefecture, is a breathtaking 8-kilometer stretch of coastline within Sanriku Fukko National Park. Often called the 'Alps of the Ocean,' it features dramatic, 200-meter-high cliffs, uniquely shaped rock formations, and sea caves.

The Tojinbo Cliffs are a spectacular, 1-kilometer stretch of rugged basalt cliffs in Fukui Prefecture, Japan. Famous for their rare, hexagonal columnar rock formations, these dramatic precipices tower up to 30 meters above the crashing waves of the Sea of Japan.

The easternmost edge of the Byobugaura cliffs in Choshi, Japan, offers stunning, untouched views of ancient volcanic and marine strata. Often called the 'Dover of the East', these majestic, steep bluffs stretch for 10 kilometers and are accessible via a scenic walking promenade right by the water.

The Jogasaki Coast is a stunning, rugged 9-kilometer stretch of the Izu Peninsula in Shizuoka, Japan. Formed 4,000 years ago by cooling lava flows from Mount Omuro, it features dramatic cliffs, maritime forests, and the iconic Kadowaki Suspension Bridge.

Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park is a diverse, volcanic wonderland famous for Mount Fuji, the Fuji Five Lakes, Hakone's hot springs, and the subtropical Izu Islands. Located just hours from Tokyo, it is Japan's most visited national park, seamlessly blending sacred peaks, rugged coastlines, and rich biodiversity.
Forested national park in the Japanese Alps featuring hiking trails, lodging & picturesque views.

The Hozugawa River Boat Cruise (Hozugawa Kudari) is a 16-kilometer (10-mile), two-hour traditional wooden boat journey from Kameoka to the Arashiyama district in Kyoto. Guided by charismatic boatmen, it blends breathtaking seasonal scenery and mild rapids with humor and historical storytelling.

Akiyoshido Cave is Japan’s largest and longest limestone cave, stretching 100 meters underground at the base of the Akiyoshidai Quasi-National Park in Yamaguchi Prefecture. Carved over 300 million years, it features a constant 17°C (62°F) climate and awe-inspiring underground cathedrals, illuminated waterfalls, and ancient rock formations.

Ryusendo Cave is one of Japan's three great limestone caves, located in Iwaizumi, Iwate Prefecture. Designated as a national natural monument, it features extensive caverns with striking stalactites and vivid, highly transparent underground lakes illuminated in a signature, mystical 'Dragon Blue'. While the explored cave system is estimated to be over 5,000 meters long, a 700-meter path is open to visitors, equipped with elevated walkways and bridges.

Universal Studios Japan is one of the world's most popular and heavily attended theme parks. Attractions include Super Nintendo World (Mario Kart: Koopa's Challenge ride), The Wizarding World of Harry Potter (Harry Potter and the Forbidden Journey ride), Thrilling Coasters (Flying Dinosaur, Hollywood Dream), Anime & Seasonal Events (rotating offerings from Demon Slayer, One Piece, and Monster Hunter), and Universal Wonderland (family-friendly area with Snoopy, Hello Kitty, and Elmo).

B-PUMP Tokyo Akihabara is a massive, four-story bouldering gym located just minutes from Akihabara and Suehirocho stations. Renowned for its world-class route setting and welcoming vibe, it caters to everyone from complete beginners to pros, and even features a dedicated rooftop climbing wall.

Widely regarded as one of the hardest and most elite bouldering gyms in the world, Tokyo's B-Pump Ogikubo is a mecca for climbers. Located just 30 seconds on foot from Ogikubo Station, it features creative route-setting, a massive replica wall modeling South Africa's Rocklands, and a fully stocked pro shop. B-Pump Ogikubo has a reputation for 'stiff' grading; climbs here frequently feel 2-3 grades harder than in US or European gyms.

B-PUMP Yokohama is a premier bouldering gym located just a 10-minute walk from Yokohama Station. Featuring renovated, world-class routes, the facility challenges everyone from first-timers to seasoned experts with Japanese martial arts-style grading and exceptional routesetting.

The Mount Fuji Panoramic Ropeway ascends Mount Tenjo from Lake Kawaguchiko, offering a breathtaking 3-minute ride to an observation deck. At the top, visitors enjoy unobstructed, panoramic views of Mount Fuji and the lake, alongside thematic statues from the Japanese folktale 'Kachi-Kachi-Yama'.

The Hakone Ropeway is a 4-kilometer aerial lift offering spectacular 30-minute journeys across the Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park. Connecting Sounzan Station with Togendai Station, the route glides over the volcanic Owakudani Valley and offers stunning views of Mt. Fuji and Lake Ashinoko. A popular and attractive way of visiting Hakone is by circling the region with five different means of transport (train, cablecar, ropeway, boat and bus), using the Hakone Free Pass. The round course outlined below starts and ends in Hakone-Yumoto and circles the Hakone region in the counter-clockwise direction. Of course, it is also possible to follow the course in the opposite direction or to start and end somewhere different.

Hakone is a mountainous hot spring region known for its secluded ryokan, forested valleys, and views of Mt. Fuji. Unlike traditional compact onsen towns, Hakone is spread across several villages connected by scenic railways and winding mountain roads. The experience centers on retreating into a ryokan for private baths, kaiseki meals, tatami rooms, and quiet mountain scenery rather than exploring a preserved historic district. Huge range from ultra-luxury ryokan to traditional inns. Private baths, kaiseki dinners, Mt. Fuji views if weather cooperates.
Probably the quintessential ryokan town. Walkable canals, yukata culture, public bath hopping, wooden inns. Very easy from Kyoto/Osaka.
Traditional hot spring town in the mountains between Nagoya and Takayama. Relaxed and less international.

Kusatsu, a town in Japan’s Kantō region, is known for its hot-spring resorts. At its center is the spring’s source, Yubatake, with its gushing, steaming waters. Nearby is the traditional Goza no Yu bathhouse. In the west, Mt. Kusatsu-Shirane is an active volcano whose summit is home to the acidic green waters of Yugama lake. To the southwest is Kazawa Yunomaru Plateau, with its sprawling, seasonal azalea fields.
Tiny Taisho-era street with gas lamps and snow-covered wooden ryokan. Almost unfairly photogenic.
Stylish countryside onsen town with upscale ryokan, cafes, and mountain scenery. More relaxed/artsy than historic. Nearby cave.
Deep forest atmosphere, outdoor baths, quiet roads, very cohesive aesthetic. One of the best ryokan-focused destinations in Japan.

Aokigahara, also known as the 'Sea of Trees' (Jukai), is a dense, 35-square-kilometer forest at the northwestern base of Mount Fuji in Japan. Thriving on hardened lava from an 864 CE eruption, the untamed wilderness is famous for its haunting isolation, porous volcanic rock that absorbs sound, and its tragic reputation as a destination for suicide.

Odaiba is a futuristic, man-made entertainment and shopping island in Tokyo Bay. Connected to central Tokyo by the iconic Rainbow Bridge, it offers a blend of expansive waterfront parks, massive malls, eccentric museums, and stunning views of the city skyline.

A traditional teahouse here offers tea ceremonies that blend quiet ritual with precise movements, from whisking the matcha to the choreography of ‘turning the bowl,’ the practice of showing off a bowl’s most aesthetically pleasing side to the guest.
(Tabelog 3.54) Located on the 2nd floor of the Ginza Pola building, an elegant shop offering a selection of traditional Japanese sweets & tableware, plus a tea salon.
(Tabelog 3.44) Brewpub with traditional interior located inside an old sake shop in historical Yanaka district
(Tabelog 3.54) A butcher shop that is known for its menchi katsu, seasoned ground beef patties covered in panko and deep-fried. Also on the second floor a 3.54 rated steakhouse
(Tabelog 3.66, 1 Michelin star) A hand-made soba noodle shop, no reservations accepted, closes once soba runs out.

(Tabelog 3.68, 1 Michelin star) This affordable Michelin-starred lunch is centered on sardines. In the standout dish, the small fish are simmered and served over ochazuke, a hot, porridgelike soup made from bonito-based dashi broth and rice.
This bakery serves market-fresh breads and pastries - think ginger scones and peanut butter-miso cookies - alongside a hearty brunch menu.

(Tabelog 3.49) The mom-and-pop soba shop has a retro, nostalgic vibe. A must-try is the cold soba, thin buckwheat noodles, with crispy tempura, lightly battered and fried seafood or vegetables. It offers a simple experience that’s authentic and flavorful. Cash only.

(Tabelog 3.69) A favorite of locals where soy-rich ramen meets spicy tantanmen (a Japanese take on Sichuan-style sesame and chili noodle soup), all served with mellow soundtrack music by Nujabes, a famed Japanese record producer, D.J., composer and arranger who was the older brother of Usagi’s owner. Open only for lunch, and cash only.

(Tabelog 3.46) A neighborhood institution since 1923, this restaurant serves golden, sesame-oil-fried tempura with precision. From tiger prawns to seasonal vegetables, it’s a crispy and flavorful tradition.

(Tabelog 3.68) [also in Ginza] The star at this upscale establishment is teppanyaki, a Japanese style of cooking in which ingredients such as vegetables and wagyu are grilled on a flat iron griddle. Or perhaps the star is the dessert lounge serving expertly crafted sweets like Japanese-style cakes and seasonal fruit parfaits?

(Tabelog 3.49) Sate your appetite with inventive varieties of tempura, such as salmon roe nested on roasted seaweed, at this restaurant. It serves unorthodox tempura with wine pairings in a setting beneath an old train viaduct.

(Tabelog 3.49) This casual 25-seat spot specializes in Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, layered savory pancakes filled with cabbage, noodles and other seasonal ingredients. Along its perimeter are 10 stools from which patrons can watch their okonomiyaki of choice get made, according to T Magazine.

(Tabelog 3.23) Housed in a basement at the end of a residential block, this sushi restaurant serves blowout omakase sushi at a seven-seat counter, along with fresh fish, squid tentacles and daily market finds.

(Tabelog 3.68) This legendary sushi restaurant is celebrated for edomae sushi, fresh Tokyo Bay seafood atop bite-size, vinegary rice. The seafood is so fresh you might find yourself eating a live shrimp.

(Tabelog 3.56) A tonkatsu chain with multiple locations. A much-loved local chain, Katsukichi specializes in thick and hearty tonkatsu, a breaded and deep-fried pork cutlet. But in the winter, diners come for a seasonal specialty: kaki-furai, or deep-fried oysters.

Nakamise is the most famous shopping street in Asakusa. The street runs for 250 meters on the main approach to Sensoji Temple and is lined on both sides with shops selling traditional souvenirs, snacks, and sweets.
Designed as a ‘library in a forest’ concept, elegant bookstore complex with music, DVD & vintage book sections, plus a plush cafe & lounge.

Golden Gai is a compact nightlife district in Tokyo’s Shinjuku ward, famous for its maze of narrow alleyways lined with hundreds of tiny bars. Located on the edge of Kabukichō, it preserves the retro charm of postwar Tokyo and remains one of the city’s most atmospheric drinking areas. Typical hours: 7 p.m.–4 a.m. (many open late). Average cover charge: ¥500–¥1,500
